original_hypothesis.title: Secondary Royalties Not Enforced On-Chain
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: MinterContract allows artists to propose secondary royalty splits and addresses, but these are never used. NextGenCore implements ERC2981 using a single global default royalty address, completely ignoring the collection-specific secondary configurations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The protocol creates an expectation that secondary royalties will be split between artists and the team according to on-chain configurations. However, the ERC2981 implementation points to a single address, meaning the splits are either non-functional or rely entirely on an off-chain trusted splitter.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _setDefaultRoyalty sets a global royalty, ignoring collection-specific secondary splits stored in MinterContract

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: ERC2981 royaltyInfo returns the global default address instead of the configured artist/team splits
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Secondary royalties flow to a single address, bypassing the on-chain split logic
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Artists may not receive their intended secondary royalties if the global address does not properly route funds off-chain

economic_questions: Is the global royalty address a smart contract splitter that reads the MinterContract state?
If not, how are artists guaranteed their secondary royalties?
validation_requirements: Check ERC2981 royaltyInfo output for different collections
Verify absence of secondary payout functions in MinterContract

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Breach of trust regarding royalty distribution

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All secondary royalties generated by the protocol

validation_result.impact_summary: Secondary royalty splits and addresses stored in MinterContract are never used; ERC2981 returns a single hardcoded global royalty address, making collection-specific secondary configurations non-functional dead state.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The MinterContract provides an elaborate workflow for configuring secondary royalty splits (setPrimaryAndSecondarySplits), proposing secondary addresses (proposeSecondaryAddressesAndPercentages), and approving them (acceptAddressesAndPercentages). However, NextGenCore's ERC2981 implementation only uses _setDefaultRoyalty with a single global address. There is no _setTokenRoyalty call anywhere in the codebase, no per-collection royalty configuration in ERC2981, and critically, no function anywhere in the system that reads the secondary split/address data to distribute secondary royalty proceeds. The payArtist function only distributes primary sale proceeds using primary addresses. The secondary configuration data (collectionRoyaltiesSecondarySplits, collectionArtistSecondaryAddresses) is write-only dead state that is never consumed by any payment logic.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The constructor in NextGenCore.sol hardcodes _setDefaultRoyalty(0x1B1289E34Fe05019511d7b436a5138F361904df0, 690), setting a single global royalty recipient at 6.9%. The setDefaultRoyalties function allows admin to change this but still only sets a global default. No _setTokenRoyalty is ever called. In MinterContract.sol, secondary splits are stored via setPrimaryAndSecondarySplits and proposeSecondaryAddressesAndPercentages, but these mappings (collectionRoyaltiesSecondarySplits, collectionArtistSecondaryAddresses) are only read by the view functions retrieveSecondarySplits and retrieveSecondaryAddressesAndPercentages - never by any payment distribution function.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Hardcodes a single global royalty recipient address for ALL tokens across ALL collections, ignoring any collection-specific secondary royalty configuration.

Only mechanism to configure ERC2981 royalties - sets a single global default, with no per-collection or per-token royalty support. Collection-specific secondary splits stored in MinterContract are never referenced.

Stores secondary split percentages per collection that are never consumed by any payment distribution function. The collectionRoyaltiesSecondarySplits mapping is only read by the view function retrieveSecondarySplits.

Stores secondary royalty recipient addresses per collection. These addresses are never used by any payment distribution function - they exist only as dead state readable via retrieveSecondaryAddressesAndPercentages.

calling_sequence: 1. Marketplace calls royaltyInfo(tokenId, salePrice) on NextGenCore
2. ERC2981._defaultRoyaltyInfo is returned (set in constructor to 0x1B1289E34Fe05019511d7b436a5138F361904df0, 690 bps)
3. Returns (globalAddress, salePrice * 690 / 10000) for ALL tokens regardless of collection
4. Secondary split data in MinterContract (collectionRoyaltiesSecondarySplits, collectionArtistSecondaryAddresses) is never consulted
5. No on-chain function exists to distribute received secondary royalties according to stored splits
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a marketplace queries ERC2981 royaltyInfo for any token, the response is always the global default royalty address and BPS set via _setDefaultRoyalty. The per-collection secondary royalty configuration stored in MinterContract (via setPrimaryAndSecondarySplits, proposeSecondaryAddressesAndPercentages, acceptAddressesAndPercentages) is completely disconnected from the ERC2981 implementation. There is no bridge between these two systems - no function reads secondary addresses to distribute royalty payments, and no _setTokenRoyalty call maps collections to their specific royalty recipients.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script (fixturesDeployment.js) deploys NextGenCore with constructor that hardcodes _setDefaultRoyalty(0x1B1289E34Fe05019511d7b436a5138F361904df0, 690). No subsequent calls to configure per-collection royalties are shown.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: This is not an attack per se but a design defect. The protocol creates an elaborate on-chain workflow for configuring secondary royalty splits (artists propose addresses/percentages, admin approves them), implying that secondary royalties will be distributed according to these splits. However, ERC2981 returns a single global address for all tokens. If the global address is not a smart contract splitter that reads MinterContract state (which there is no evidence of in the codebase), then secondary royalties flow to a single address with no on-chain guarantee of proper distribution to artists. Artists who go through the proposal/approval workflow gain no on-chain enforcement of their secondary royalty share.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The secondary royalty configuration infrastructure (4 functions, 4 storage mappings, associated structs) is non-functional dead code that creates a false expectation of on-chain royalty enforcement. Artists may believe their secondary royalty splits are enforced on-chain when they are not. All secondary royalties flow to a single global address with distribution relying entirely on off-chain trust. The actual royalty recipient address (0x1B1289E34Fe05019511d7b436a5138F361904df0) appears to be a team-controlled address, giving the team unilateral control over secondary royalty distribution.
recommended_mitigations: Implement per-collection royalty settings using ERC2981's _setTokenRoyalty or a custom override of royaltyInfo that reads from MinterContract's secondary configuration
Deploy a royalty splitter contract per collection that reads the on-chain secondary split configuration and automatically distributes received funds
Add a secondary royalty distribution function in MinterContract analogous to payArtist that distributes secondary royalties from a receiving contract
At minimum, document clearly that secondary royalty splits are informational only and not enforced on-chain
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy full system via fixturesDeployment.js. 2. Create collection and configure it. 3. Call setPrimaryAndSecondarySplits with artist 60% / team 40% secondary split. 4. Call proposeSecondaryAddressesAndPercentages with specific artist addresses. 5. Call acceptAddressesAndPercentages to approve. 6. Call royaltyInfo on NextGenCore for any token in the collection. 7. Observe returned address is the global default (0x1B1289E34Fe05019511d7b436a5138F361904df0), not any of the configured secondary addresses. 8. Verify no function exists to distribute funds from the global royalty address to the configured secondary split recipients.
uncertainties: The global royalty address (0x1B1289E34Fe05019511d7b436a5138F361904df0) could potentially be a smart contract that reads MinterContract state off-chain or uses a separate mechanism - but there is no evidence of this in the provided codebase
The admin could update the default royalty to a per-collection splitter contract via setDefaultRoyalties, but this would be global not per-collection
The secondary configuration may be intentionally informational/off-chain only, but the code creates on-chain state that implies enforcement
additional_testing_needed: Verify the nature of address 0x1B1289E34Fe05019511d7b436a5138F361904df0 - is it an EOA or a contract?
Test royaltyInfo returns for tokens from different collections to confirm same global address
Search for any off-chain infrastructure that may bridge MinterContract secondary config to actual royalty distribution
